2.5.a.info:
The root bridge is the single switch elected as the reference point for the entire spanning-tree topology inside a VLAN. Every non-root switch measures its path cost toward the root, and every port role (Root/Designated/Alternate) is defined in relation to it. Pick the wrong root and your whole L2 traffic pattern gets ugly — traffic flows through access switches instead of through the distribution layer.

Who wins? The switch with the lowest Bridge ID. Bridge ID = Priority (with Extended System ID / VLAN number baked in) + base MAC address. Lower priority wins first; if priority ties, the switch with the lowest MAC wins. Default priority on every Cisco switch is 32768, so without intervention the oldest switch (lowest MAC) typically becomes root — which is almost never where you want it.

Root-bridge-only behavior: The root generates BPDUs every 2 seconds (hello timer). All of its ports are in Designated role (it doesn't need a root port — it IS the root). Other switches forward/modify BPDUs but do not originate them from the root's perspective.

Forcing a good root: spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary lowers priority to 24576, and spanning-tree vlan 10 root secondary sets 28672 as the backup. For explicit control: spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096 (must be a multiple of 4096). Verify with show spanning-tree vlan 10 — look for the line "This bridge is the root".

Exam trap: Per-VLAN Spanning Tree means every VLAN can have a different root bridge. Cisco's Rapid PVST+ runs one RSTP instance per VLAN. The election repeats independently in each VLAN.

children.name: Non-root Switch C (has Root Port + Alternate)

hack.memory: Root bridge = 'King of the VLAN.' Lowest Bridge ID wins the crown. Priority compared first (default 32768); MAC address is the tiebreaker. The king generates BPDUs every 2s, has NO root port (all ports are Designated), and everyone else measures cost toward him.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer build a triangle of three 2960 switches. Run 'show spanning-tree vlan 1' on all three — the one with 'This bridge is the root' won by MAC tiebreaker (priorities default-tied at 32769 for VLAN 1). Now on a different switch run 'spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary' and re-check — priority drops to 24576 and root moves. Then run 'spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 4096' on a third switch to override even the macro.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20-21 (STP Part 1 & 2) and Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. The exam will show a topology + Bridge IDs and ask which switch is root — priority first, MAC tiebreaker. Expect at least one 'force this switch to be root' command question.

2.5.a.info:
The Root Port (RP) is the single port on each non-root switch that provides the lowest-cost path toward the root bridge. Every non-root switch has exactly one root port — no more, no less. The root bridge itself has zero root ports (it's already the root). Root ports are always in Forwarding state.

Selection algorithm (apply in order):

  • Lowest root path cost — sum of STP costs of every outgoing link between this switch and the root (10G=2, 1G=4, 100M=19, 10M=100).
  • Tie → lowest sender (upstream neighbor) Bridge ID.
  • Tie → lowest sender port priority (default 128, increments of 16).
  • Tie → lowest sender port number (Gi0/1 beats Gi0/2).

Worked example: SwitchB has two uplinks. Gi0/1 → 1 Gbps direct to root = cost 4. Gi0/2 → 1 Gbps to SwitchC → 1 Gbps to root = cost 4 + 4 = 8. RP = Gi0/1. Gi0/2 becomes an Alternate Port (discarding, backup).

Verification: show spanning-tree vlan 10 shows Role = Root and Cost to root in the Root ID section. show spanning-tree root gives a compact per-VLAN summary with the root port column.

RSTP twist: When the root port fails, RSTP's Alternate Port can transition to Forwarding in well under a second via the proposal/agreement mechanism — no 30-second wait like classic STP.

hack.memory: Root Port = 'shortest road to the king.' ONE per non-root switch, ALWAYS forwarding. Rule chain: lowest ROOT PATH COST → lowest sender BID → lowest sender port priority (128 default) → lowest sender port number. Root bridge has no RP — he's already home.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer build a square of four switches: root at top-left. Mix link speeds (one 100M link so cost = 19, others 1G cost = 4). On each non-root switch run 'show spanning-tree' and identify the Root Port — verify by summing link costs on paper first, then confirming. Change one link to 10Gbps (cost 2) and watch the RP move.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 21 walks the selection algorithm. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam will hand you a diagram with link speeds and ask 'what is SwA's root port?' — you must do the cost math and apply tiebreakers.

2.5.a.info:
A Designated Port (DP) is the port on each network segment (each link between two switches, or between a switch and endpoints) that provides the best path toward the root bridge for that segment. There is exactly one Designated Port per segment. Designated ports are in Forwarding state — they're the ports that actually carry user traffic.

Key rule: On the root bridge, every port is a Designated Port. The root is the reference point, so every segment touching the root naturally has the root as its best path toward the root.

DP election on non-root segments: The two switches on either end of a segment compare their root path cost. The lower cost wins — that side's port becomes the DP. If costs tie, lowest Bridge ID wins; then lowest port priority; then lowest port number. The losing side's port becomes an Alternate Port and is blocked (discarding).

Mental model: For any segment, ask "which side of this wire is closer to the root?" The closer side gets the Designated Port; the farther side gets a Root Port or Alternate Port depending on whether it's their single best path.

Verification: show spanning-tree lists port roles in the bottom table. Desg = Designated. All root-bridge ports will show Desg. On a non-root access switch, the port going down to PCs is Desg (it's facing a segment with only end hosts — the switch is trivially the closest path to root for that segment).

hack.memory: Designated Port = 'this side of the wire is closer to the king.' ONE per segment, always forwarding. On the root bridge, ALL ports are Designated. To find a DP on a mid-network segment: compare the two switches' root path costs — lower cost side wins.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer build any STP topology. On 'show spanning-tree', mark every port on every switch: is it Root, Desg, or Altn? Confirm that every segment has exactly ONE Desg port. Then force a different switch to be root (priority 4096) and watch DPs flip sides on some segments.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 21. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam shows a topology and asks 'identify all designated ports.' Shortcut: all ports on the root are DPs; on every other segment, the switch with the lower cost to root has the DP.

2.5.a.info:
The Alternate Port is an RSTP concept for a port that hears a better BPDU from another switch — meaning it has a viable backup path to the root bridge, but not the best path. Because forwarding on this port would create a loop, RSTP places it in Discarding state.

Why it exists: In classic 802.1D STP, redundant ports simply sat in Blocking and had to go through 30-50 seconds of Listening + Learning before forwarding when the root port failed. RSTP renamed this role 'Alternate' and built in instant failover — if the primary Root Port fails, the Alternate transitions to Forwarding in well under a second because RSTP already knows it's a safe backup path.

Difference from Backup Port: Alternate Port = backup path via a different switch (the common case). Backup Port = backup for a Designated Port on the same switch (only happens on a shared hub segment with two ports into it — rare).

Identifying Alternate ports: Run show spanning-tree. A port with Role = Altn and Sts = BLK is an Alternate in discarding. You'll see this on the second uplink of any switch with dual uplinks to redundant root paths.

Exam sanity check: Classic STP used 'Non-Designated / Blocking.' RSTP split this into 'Alternate' (backup root-port-ish) and 'Backup' (backup designated-port-ish). Modern switches default to Rapid PVST+, so Alternate is the term you'll see on real output.

states: Receives inferior BPDU path → becomes Alternate
Root Port fails → RSTP promotes Alt

hack.memory: Alternate Port = 'standby root port.' Hears a better path to root via another neighbor, so it sits in Discarding (blocked) as a hot backup. RSTP flips it to Forwarding in milliseconds when the real Root Port dies. Classic STP just called this 'Blocking' and made you wait 30s.
hack.practice: Build a triangle: root plus SwA and SwB, both dual-connected. On SwB, check 'show spanning-tree' — one port is Root (to root), one is Alternate (to SwA, blocked). Shut the Root Port interface and immediately re-run 'show spanning-tree' — the Alternate should have transitioned to Root/Forwarding without the long STP wait.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22 (RSTP) and Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'which RSTP port role provides a backup path to the root bridge via a different switch?' = Alternate. Know the Altn/Back distinction.

2.5.a.info:
The Backup Port is a rare RSTP role: a port that backs up a Designated Port on the same switch. It's only possible when a single switch has two ports into the same collision/shared segment — i.e., both ports hang off the same hub or (in modern gear) are wired to each other through an odd looped cable on a shared medium.

Why it's rare: Almost all modern Ethernet is full-duplex point-to-point between two switches, not shared. Shared-medium situations with hubs have essentially disappeared in enterprise networks. You'll rarely see a Backup port on real gear.

State: Always Discarding. It only activates (transitions to Forwarding) if the primary Designated Port on the same switch fails. Unlike Alternate Port failover (which covers root-port failure via a different switch), Backup Port covers designated-port failure via a second port on the same switch.

Identify in output: Role = Back in show spanning-tree. Extremely uncommon to see in practice.

Exam scope: The CCNA may include 'Backup' in a list of RSTP port roles and ask you to differentiate it from Alternate. Memorize: Alternate = backup via another switch. Backup = backup on the same switch (shared segment only).

right.label: Backup Port
items: Backup for Desg Port on SAME switch
Discarding → Forwarding if that DP fails

hack.memory: Backup Port = 'second port into the same hub/segment, same switch.' Almost mythical in modern full-duplex networks. Alternate backs up via another switch; Backup backs up via another port on the same switch.
hack.practice: You can't easily lab a Backup Port in Packet Tracer without a hub. Instead, memorize the distinction on a flashcard: Altn = different switch, Back = same switch. That's all the exam will ask.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22 mentions Backup briefly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10 documents it. Exam rarely asks beyond 'name all four RSTP port roles: Root, Designated, Alternate, Backup.'

2.5.a.term: Root bridge has no Root Port
2.5.a.info:
A direct consequence of the port-role definitions: the root bridge itself has zero Root Ports. All of its ports are Designated. This is an easy exam trap — you might see a scenario that asks 'how many root ports does the root bridge have?' and the answer is zero.

Why: A Root Port is defined as 'the port with the lowest-cost path to the root bridge.' On the root bridge, every path to the root has cost 0 (you're already there). There's no meaningful choice to make. Instead, every port on the root is the 'closest side to root' for its segment, so every port is a Designated Port.

Practical implication: The root bridge is the only switch in the topology that originates BPDUs — it sends them out of every DP every 2 seconds (hello timer). Non-root switches receive these BPDUs on their Root Port, update their own Bridge ID info if needed, and re-advertise down to their own DPs.

Verification trick: On a switch you suspect is root, run show spanning-tree vlan 10. If the Root ID section literally says "This bridge is the root", confirmed. Also check the port table — no port will show Role = Root.

Teaching tie-in: This is why forcing the root matters. If you leave defaults, some random access-layer switch becomes root (lowest MAC), and suddenly all of its ports are Designated and traffic flows through it — often creating suboptimal paths across your campus.

params.root: Root Bridge — ALL ports = Desg, ZERO Root Ports

hack.memory: Root bridge = all Designated, NO Root Port. The king doesn't need a road to his own castle. Every port on the root is the 'closest side to root' for its segment.
hack.practice: In any Packet Tracer STP lab, find the root bridge ('This bridge is the root'). On it, run 'show spanning-tree' and confirm EVERY port shows Role = Desg. Then on any non-root switch, count the Root Ports — exactly one.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 21. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam trick question: 'how many RPs on the root bridge?' = 0. Also: 'which switch generates BPDUs?' = only the root (non-root switches forward/update them).

2.5.b.info:
RSTP (Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol, IEEE 802.1w) is the modern replacement for classic 802.1D STP. It solves the #1 complaint about classic STP: slow convergence. Classic STP takes 30-50 seconds to recover from a topology change. RSTP converges in under a second in most scenarios.

Three core improvements:

  • Three port states instead of five (Discarding / Learning / Forwarding — no more Disabled/Blocking/Listening).
  • Proposal/Agreement handshake — adjacent switches negotiate port transitions directly using BPDUs, bypassing the classic 15-second forward-delay timer.
  • New port roles (Alternate and Backup) whose behavior is pre-computed so failover can be nearly instant.

Backward compatible: RSTP switches and 802.1D switches can coexist on the same network. An RSTP port that detects an 802.1D neighbor falls back to classic STP timer-based behavior on that specific port only, while other ports stay in rapid mode.

Cisco flavor: Cisco runs Rapid PVST+ by default on modern switches — basically RSTP with one instance per VLAN (same idea as PVST+ relative to classic STP). Configure with spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst. Verify with show spanning-tree — the first line will say 'Spanning tree enabled protocol rstp'.

Exam focus: Know that RSTP = 802.1w, classic STP = 802.1D, Cisco default = Rapid PVST+. Understand why RSTP converges faster (proposal/agreement + pre-computed alternate roles, not timer waits).

hack.memory: RSTP = 'Rapid STP = 802.1w.' Three states (Discarding/Learning/Forwarding), sub-second convergence via proposal/agreement. Cisco default = Rapid PVST+ (RSTP per-VLAN). Classic STP = 802.1D, 5 states, 30-50s delay — effectively obsolete.
hack.practice: On a Cisco switch run 'show spanning-tree summary' — note 'Switch is in rapid-pvst mode'. Change it to classic with 'spanning-tree mode pvst' and re-check. In Packet Tracer, both modes are supported; compare link-failure recovery times — RSTP reconverges visibly faster.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what IEEE standard is RSTP?' = 802.1w. 'What is Cisco's default STP mode?' = Rapid PVST+.

2.5.b.info:
Discarding is RSTP's default 'not active' state. A port in Discarding is not forwarding data frames and not learning MAC addresses. It is, however, still processing BPDUs — that's how the switch knows whether the port's role should change.

What it replaces: Discarding collapses three classic STP states into one:

  • Disabled (admin shutdown — port is down)
  • Blocking (port is up, hearing BPDUs, but not forwarding — standard redundant-path state)
  • Listening (transitional state after topology change, 15 seconds in classic STP)

All three had the same user-visible behavior — no forwarding, no learning — so RSTP merged them for simplicity. Alternate Ports and Backup Ports live in Discarding; ports transitioning from down to up also start in Discarding.

Identify in output: show spanning-tree column Sts shows BLK for Discarding (Cisco kept the abbreviation from classic STP even though the state name changed).

Exam trap: A question may ask 'which classic STP states are combined into Discarding in RSTP?' — answer is Disabled + Blocking + Listening. Not Learning (Learning stayed as its own state in RSTP).

hack.memory: Discarding = 'all three do-nothing states rolled into one.' Disabled + Blocking + Listening → Discarding. No forward, no learn, but still processes BPDUs. Cisco shows it as 'BLK' in 'show spanning-tree'.
hack.practice: On a switch with a blocked redundant uplink, run 'show spanning-tree' — the blocked port shows Role=Altn, Sts=BLK. That BLK is RSTP's Discarding. Then admin-shutdown the port and re-check — still BLK (the Disabled and Blocking states look identical from here).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'which three classic STP states became Discarding in RSTP?' = Disabled, Blocking, Listening.

2.5.b.info:
Learning is a transitional state where the port is not yet forwarding user data but is examining incoming frame source MACs and populating the CAM table. This prewarms the MAC address table so that when the port flips to Forwarding, the switch can make unicast decisions instead of flooding every frame.

Duration: In classic STP, Learning was a fixed 15-second timer (Forward Delay). In RSTP, Learning is still present but is bypassed entirely on point-to-point edge ports and for ports that win a proposal/agreement exchange — the port jumps from Discarding straight to Forwarding in milliseconds.

Why it matters practically: Without the Learning phase, when a formerly blocked port becomes forwarding, the switch would flood every incoming frame out all VLAN ports (because no MACs are learned yet). The Learning state lets the CAM populate first, minimizing the flood burst.

Identify in output: show spanning-tree shows Sts = LRN. You'll only catch it briefly during a topology change, blink-and-you-miss-it on RSTP.

Exam point: Learning = populate MAC table but do NOT forward data. It exists in both classic STP and RSTP (it's one of the three RSTP states).

hack.memory: Learning = 'eyes open, mouth shut.' Reads source MACs to build the CAM table but doesn't forward any frames yet. 15s timer in classic STP; near-instant in RSTP. Prevents the flood burst when a port starts forwarding.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, shut a blocked port's primary path so STP must reconverge. In simulation mode step through — the previously blocked port will flicker LRN briefly before FWD. Faster in RSTP mode than classic PVST.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'in which state does a port build its MAC table but not forward data?' = Learning.

2.5.b.info:
Forwarding is the fully operational port state — the port forwards data frames and learns MAC addresses. This is the target state for every Root Port and every Designated Port in the topology.

How a port reaches Forwarding:

  • Classic STP: Disabled → Blocking → Listening (15s) → Learning (15s) → Forwarding. 30-50s total including max-age when coming out of Blocking.
  • RSTP: Discarding → Learning → Forwarding. With proposal/agreement on a point-to-point link, this can happen in well under a second. On an edge/PortFast port, it's immediate.

Alternate/Backup ports never reach Forwarding in steady state — they stay in Discarding as hot standbys. They only transition to Forwarding if they get promoted (e.g., Root Port fails and the Alternate takes over).

Verification: show spanning-tree column Sts = FWD. show spanning-tree interface gi0/1 detail shows the full forwarding state, counters, and timers for a specific port.

Exam: 'What RSTP state forwards frames and learns MACs?' = Forwarding. This is the target state for RP and DP.

hack.memory: Forwarding = 'fully on' — forwards frames AND learns MACs. Target state for Root Ports and Designated Ports. Alternate/Backup ports stay in Discarding unless promoted.
hack.practice: Pick any working topology in Packet Tracer — on 'show spanning-tree', every port showing Role=Root or Role=Desg should have Sts=FWD. Every port showing Role=Altn should have Sts=BLK. That's the full state machine in two commands.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Guaranteed exam: 'which state both forwards frames and learns MACs?' = Forwarding.

2.5.b.info:
The Proposal/Agreement mechanism is how RSTP achieves sub-second convergence on point-to-point links. Instead of waiting for timers (forward delay, max age), two adjacent RSTP switches negotiate a port's transition to Forwarding via a quick BPDU exchange.

The exchange (3 steps):

  • Switch A wants to make a port Designated/Forwarding. It sends a BPDU with the Proposal bit set.
  • Switch B receives the Proposal. It syncs — temporarily blocks its own non-edge ports to prevent loops — then sends back an Agreement BPDU.
  • Switch A sees the Agreement and immediately transitions its port to Forwarding. Switch B re-enables its ports in an orderly sequence, propagating the proposal/agreement downstream.

Why it's fast: No 15-second listening, no 15-second learning — the protocol explicitly confirms both sides agree the port is loop-free before enabling forwarding. Full reconvergence on a stable network is typically <1 second.

Requires point-to-point full-duplex links. Shared/half-duplex links revert to timer-based behavior. Modern switch-to-switch is almost always full-duplex, so proposal/agreement is the norm.

Exam: Know that proposal/agreement is RSTP's fast-convergence mechanism and it replaces the timer-based transitions of classic STP.

hack.memory: Proposal/Agreement = 'Do you agree this port is safe to forward?' → 'Yes.' Three-BPDU handshake replaces 30s of classic STP timers. Requires point-to-point full-duplex. If shared/half-duplex, RSTP falls back to timers.
hack.practice: Hard to observe directly in Packet Tracer since the handshake takes milliseconds. Just internalize the concept: timer-free, sub-second, requires full-duplex P2P. Mention in any exam question explaining why RSTP > classic STP.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what mechanism allows RSTP to converge without waiting for timers?' = proposal/agreement. 'What link type does it require?' = point-to-point full-duplex.

2.5.b.info:
Rapid PVST+ is Cisco's default STP mode on modern Catalyst switches. It runs one RSTP (802.1w) instance per VLAN — combining RSTP's fast convergence with per-VLAN spanning tree's independent topology per VLAN. Cisco-proprietary because 802.1w itself doesn't specify per-VLAN instances (MST does instead).

Why per-VLAN matters: Different VLANs can have different root bridges. VLAN 10 can have SwA as root while VLAN 20 has SwB as root, letting you load-balance: VLAN 10 traffic uses the SwA-preferred path, VLAN 20 traffic uses SwB-preferred. This is Cisco's bread and butter for campus L2 design.

Configuration: spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst (global — default on modern IOS). Per-VLAN root control: spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary, spanning-tree vlan 20 root primary (on different switches). Per-VLAN priority: spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096.

Scale warning: One RSTP instance per VLAN means on a switch with 1,000 VLANs, you run 1,000 separate RSTP instances. CPU and memory overhead add up. For massive VLAN counts, MST (Multiple Spanning Tree, 802.1s) is more efficient — it groups VLANs into a few instances. The CCNA focuses on Rapid PVST+; MST is mentioned but not configured.

Verification: show spanning-tree shows each VLAN's instance. First line: 'Spanning tree enabled protocol rstp'.

hack.memory: Rapid PVST+ = 'RSTP run separately for every VLAN' and it's Cisco's default. Best of both worlds: RSTP's speed + per-VLAN flexibility (different root per VLAN for load balancing). Scales poorly with huge VLAN counts — use MST instead in that case.
hack.practice: On a Cisco switch: 'show spanning-tree summary' reveals mode (rapid-pvst). Build two VLANs in Packet Tracer and force a different root in each: 'spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary' on SwA, 'spanning-tree vlan 20 root primary' on SwB. Now traffic in VLAN 10 prefers SwA, VLAN 20 prefers SwB — classic per-VLAN load sharing.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what is the default STP mode on modern Cisco Catalyst switches?' = Rapid PVST+. 'What does per-VLAN mean?' = a separate RSTP instance per VLAN allowing different roots per VLAN.

2.5.b.info:
The headline number for the exam: RSTP converges in under a second; classic STP takes 30-50 seconds. Understanding why matters for the CCNA and real-world troubleshooting.

Classic STP timers (802.1D):

  • Hello timer = 2s (root sends BPDUs every 2 seconds)
  • Max Age = 20s (how long a port waits without hearing BPDUs before considering the current info stale)
  • Forward Delay = 15s (time spent in Listening and again in Learning)

Classic convergence scenarios:

  • Direct link failure: ~30s (15s Listening + 15s Learning to forward an alternate port)
  • Indirect link failure: ~50s (20s Max Age wait + 15s Listening + 15s Learning)

RSTP convergence: Sub-second (often 100-300ms) via proposal/agreement on P2P full-duplex links. No Max Age wait because RSTP detects BPDU loss in ~6s (3× hello) and Alternate Ports are pre-computed, so the switch knows immediately where to fail over.

Why the gap exists: Classic STP is defensive and slow — wait out timers to be absolutely sure there's no loop. RSTP is proactive — know the alternate paths, agree with neighbors, skip timers when it's safe.

Exam numbers to memorize: Hello 2s, Max Age 20s, Forward Delay 15s (classic STP timers). RSTP = seconds. Classic STP = 30s (direct) / 50s (indirect).

hack.memory: Timers to burn in: Hello=2s, MaxAge=20s, ForwardDelay=15s. Classic STP takes 30-50s to reconverge. RSTP takes under a second. '2-20-15, 30-50' — five numbers cover every STP timer question on the exam.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, set a switch to 'spanning-tree mode pvst' (classic). Shut an active link and watch the status lights — a 30-second freeze. Set back to 'spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst' and repeat — almost instant recovery. Seeing the delay live makes the number stick.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 20-22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9-10. Exam: 'what is the default Forward Delay timer?' = 15s. 'What is Max Age?' = 20s. 'Classic STP convergence time for a direct failure?' = 30s. 'RSTP convergence?' = a few seconds.

2.5.c.info:
PortFast is an STP optimization that lets a port skip the Listening and Learning phases (in classic STP) or skip the proposal/agreement wait (in RSTP) and go directly to Forwarding the moment link comes up. It is designed for access ports facing end hosts — PCs, printers, IP phones, cameras — devices that will never send BPDUs.

Problem it solves: Without PortFast, a PC plugged into a switch port has to wait ~30 seconds for the port to go Forwarding. During that wait, the PC's DHCP client typically times out, user login scripts fail, or 'the network is broken' support tickets pour in. PortFast eliminates the wait.

Absolute rule: access ports only. PortFast on a switch-to-switch link is a loop-creating disaster — the port goes Forwarding before STP can detect a loop, and the network melts. This is why PortFast is always paired with BPDU Guard.

Configuration:

  • Per-interface: interface gi0/1 + spanning-tree portfast
  • Global default (applies to all access ports automatically, not trunks): spanning-tree portfast default
  • Disable on a specific interface when the global is enabled: spanning-tree portfast disable

RSTP behavior: In RSTP, a PortFast port is called an Edge Port. Same concept, different terminology. The show spanning-tree Type column will show Edge.

Exam must-know: PortFast goes on access ports. Never on trunks or switch-to-switch. Pair with BPDU Guard. Global enable = spanning-tree portfast default.

hack.memory: PortFast = 'skip the 30-second STP waiting room.' Access ports only (PCs, printers, phones). Never switch-to-switch = instant loop. Global enable = 'spanning-tree portfast default'. In RSTP it's an Edge Port.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: connect a PC to a switch port without PortFast — boot the PC and watch the amber link light for ~30s before it goes green. Shut the port, add 'spanning-tree portfast', no-shut. Now the PC connects instantly. That single side-by-side comparison drives the concept home.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'which feature skips the STP listening/learning states on an access port?' = PortFast. 'What is the RSTP name?' = Edge Port.

2.5.c.info:
Two common ways to turn on PortFast on Cisco switches, and understanding both saves you time.

Per-interface (explicit):

interface gi0/1
 switchport mode access
 spanning-tree portfast

Applies PortFast only to this specific interface. Most granular option. You'll see a warning reminding you never to put this on a switch-to-switch link.

Global default:

spanning-tree portfast default

Enables PortFast on every access port automatically. Trunk ports are NOT affected — this is the safety net. Trunks go to other switches, so PortFast would be dangerous there.

Disable it on a specific port when global is on:

interface gi0/5
 spanning-tree portfast disable

Verification: show spanning-tree interface gi0/1 portfast reveals state. show running-config interface gi0/1 shows the configured command. In show spanning-tree, the Type column shows Edge for RSTP or P2p Edge for a point-to-point edge port.

Best practice: In production, use global default + always pair with global BPDU Guard (spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default). One-liner protects every access port automatically.

hack.memory: Two flavors: 'spanning-tree portfast' on an interface = surgical. 'spanning-tree portfast default' globally = covers every access port at once, safely ignores trunks. Production: use global default + BPDU Guard default.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer configure both ways on different interfaces and verify with 'show spanning-tree interface X portfast'. Then add 'spanning-tree portfast disable' on one of the globally-enabled interfaces and confirm it falls back to normal STP behavior.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam often tests the exact command syntax — 'which command enables PortFast on all access ports by default?' = spanning-tree portfast default.

2.5.c.info:
The single most important PortFast rule: never enable PortFast on a port connected to another switch. Doing so creates an instant Layer 2 loop before STP has a chance to detect and block the port.

Why it loops: Normally, when a switch-to-switch link comes up, STP takes ~30 seconds (classic) or a few seconds (RSTP) to identify which port should be blocked. During that time, BPDUs flow, roles are calculated, and the loop-creating port is placed in Discarding. With PortFast, that evaluation is skipped. The port jumps straight to Forwarding. If it forms a loop with another path, broadcasts multiply exponentially in milliseconds — a classic broadcast storm that takes the LAN down until you physically unplug something.

The real-world trigger: A user plugs a spare switch or an unauthorized hub into a PortFast-enabled access port. Boom — instant loop. This exact scenario is why BPDU Guard was invented.

Protection pattern (always use this combo):

interface range gi0/1 - 24
 switchport mode access
spanning-tree portfast default
spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default

Now every access port has PortFast (fast boot for PCs) AND BPDU Guard (err-disable on any BPDU — if someone plugs in a switch, the port shuts down instantly instead of looping).

Exam scenario: 'What happens when PortFast is enabled on a switch-to-switch link?' = Layer 2 loop / broadcast storm. 'How do you prevent it?' = BPDU Guard.

items: Never combine PortFast with an uplink
Always pair PortFast with BPDU Guard

hack.memory: PortFast + switch-to-switch = instant L2 loop = network meltdown. Rule of thumb: PortFast is for things that don't send BPDUs. Another switch DOES send BPDUs. Pair PortFast with BPDU Guard — if a BPDU shows up, err-disable the port before damage.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer enable 'spanning-tree portfast' on a port, then connect a second switch to it WITHOUT BPDU Guard. Watch the loop form — broadcast storm lights up the topology. Now enable 'spanning-tree bpduguard enable' and retry — the port err-disables before any damage. Lesson learned in 90 seconds.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what is the risk of PortFast on a switch-to-switch link?' = L2 loop. 'What feature mitigates that risk?' = BPDU Guard.

2.5.c.info:
BPDU Guard is the safety net for PortFast. It monitors a port for incoming BPDUs — if it ever receives one, the port is immediately placed into err-disabled state (fully shut down), protecting the LAN from a rogue switch forming a loop on an access port.

Logic: Access ports should only see end hosts. End hosts don't send BPDUs. If a BPDU shows up, something is wrong — probably a user plugged in a switch, hub, or misconfigured device. Rather than let it form a loop, BPDU Guard errs on the side of total shutdown.

Configuration (two flavors):

  • Per-interface: interface gi0/1 + spanning-tree bpduguard enable
  • Global (applies to all PortFast ports): spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default

The global form only protects ports that also have PortFast enabled. So the common production combo is spanning-tree portfast default + spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default — one line each, full coverage.

Recovery from err-disable:

  • Manual: interface gi0/1 + shutdown + no shutdown
  • Automatic: errdisable recovery cause bpduguard + errdisable recovery interval 300 (recover after 300 seconds)

Verification: show interfaces status err-disabled lists err-disabled ports with reason. show errdisable recovery shows auto-recovery config.

Exam: BPDU Guard → any BPDU → err-disable. Pair with PortFast. Recovery is shut/no-shut or errdisable recovery.

hack.memory: BPDU Guard = 'if you hear a BPDU on an access port, SHOOT THE PORT.' Goes to err-disabled. Pair with PortFast always. Recover: shut/no-shut OR enable errdisable recovery for cause bpduguard.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: enable PortFast + BPDU Guard on Sw1 Gi0/1. Connect Sw2 to that port. Within a second, Gi0/1 err-disables. Check 'show interfaces status err-disabled' — reason = bpduguard. Recover: 'interface gi0/1' + 'shutdown' + 'no shutdown'. Then configure auto-recovery: 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' + 'errdisable recovery interval 60'. Retest — port auto-recovers after 60s.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what happens when a BPDU Guard port receives a BPDU?' = err-disabled. 'How do you recover?' = shut/no-shut OR errdisable recovery. 'Default enable command?' = spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default.

2.5.c.info:
When BPDU Guard (or many other features) detects a violation, it places the port in err-disabled — the port is administratively up in config but operationally down, and it will not recover on its own without intervention.

Manual recovery:

interface gi0/1
 shutdown
 no shutdown

This clears the err-disabled state and allows the port to come back up. It's simple, but if you have 50 ports err-disabled across a switch, you're doing 50 manual shuts.

Automatic recovery (global):

errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
errdisable recovery interval 300

First line tells the switch to auto-recover ports err-disabled due to BPDU Guard. Second line sets the recovery interval (default 300 seconds, configurable 30-86400). You can enable recovery for many causes: bpduguard, link-flap, security-violation (port security), psecure-violation, etc. Use errdisable recovery cause all for blanket auto-recovery (not recommended in production — too noisy).

Verification:

  • show interfaces status err-disabled — current err-disabled ports with cause
  • show errdisable recovery — which causes auto-recover, current timer
  • show errdisable detect — which causes are configured to err-disable ports

Real-world tip: Enable auto-recovery for BPDU Guard with a generous interval (300-600s). This prevents a flapping port from constantly toggling but also saves you from manually recovering every time a user's cheap switch bumps an access port.

hack.memory: err-disabled = 'port is grounded.' Get out of it by (a) manual shut/no-shut or (b) configuring errdisable recovery cause X + interval Y. Check with 'show interfaces status err-disabled'.
hack.practice: Trigger BPDU Guard in Packet Tracer (lab above). Recover manually once. Then configure 'errdisable recovery cause bpduguard' + 'errdisable recovery interval 60'. Trigger again and watch the port recover automatically after 60s. Check 'show errdisable recovery' to see the timer counting down.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'how do you recover a BPDU-Guard-triggered err-disabled port?' = shut/no-shut OR errdisable recovery. Know both commands.

2.5.d.info:
Root Guard is an STP protection feature that prevents a specific port from ever becoming a Root Port — effectively preventing any switch connected to that port from becoming the root bridge for the local switch's VLAN. It's applied to Designated Ports where you control the STP topology and don't want an outside switch to hijack the root election.

Behavior: A Designated Port with Root Guard enabled is allowed to send BPDUs, but if it ever receives a superior BPDU (one with a better Bridge ID than the current root), it moves to root-inconsistent state — effectively blocked, no data forwarding — and stays that way until the superior BPDUs stop. Recovery is automatic: remove the offending switch, and the port silently returns to normal Designated/Forwarding.

Where to apply: On a distribution switch's ports that face an access switch you administer. You want the distribution switch to be root; you don't want an access switch sneaking in as root. Also on any link to a partner/untrusted network where an external switch should never influence your internal STP.

Configuration:

interface gi0/1
 spanning-tree guard root

Verification: A port in the tripped state shows up in show spanning-tree inconsistentports and has state root-inconsistent. Syslog logs 'ROOTGUARD_BLOCK' events.

Key difference from BPDU Guard: BPDU Guard fires on any BPDU and err-disables. Root Guard fires only on superior BPDUs and root-inconsistent (not err-disabled). Root Guard auto-recovers; BPDU Guard doesn't.

items: Port remains BLK until threat gone

hack.memory: Root Guard = 'you can connect, but you can never be king.' Guards Designated Ports against superior BPDUs (a rogue lower Bridge ID). Response: root-inconsistent (blocked). Auto-recovers when the superior BPDUs stop. NOT err-disabled — different from BPDU Guard.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: make SwA root (priority 4096). On SwA's Gi0/2 toward SwC, enable 'spanning-tree guard root'. Now on SwC set a lower priority (0). SwC tries to send superior BPDUs — SwA's Gi0/2 goes root-inconsistent instead of letting SwC win. 'show spanning-tree inconsistentports' confirms. Raise SwC's priority back to default — SwA's port auto-recovers.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'which feature prevents a new root bridge election via a specific port?' = Root Guard. 'What state does the protected port enter?' = root-inconsistent.

2.5.d.info:
Root Guard recovery is automatic and hands-off — there's no command to run, no err-disable to clear. When a Root Guard-protected port stops receiving superior BPDUs (the rogue switch is unplugged, removed, or has its priority corrected), the port silently returns to its previous role (usually Designated) and resumes Forwarding.

How to trigger recovery: Remove whatever is sending the superior BPDUs. Options:

  • Physically disconnect the offending switch
  • Raise the offending switch's priority so it's no longer superior (e.g., set to 32768)
  • Disable STP on the offending port (not recommended but works)

Timing: When superior BPDUs stop, the port waits for Max Age (default 20s, classic STP) or a similar RSTP timeout, then transitions back through Listening/Learning (classic) or proposal/agreement (RSTP) to Forwarding. Total recovery is in the tens of seconds range for classic STP; faster under RSTP.

Verification:

  • While tripped: show spanning-tree inconsistentports lists the port as root-inconsistent.
  • After recovery: the port disappears from that list. show spanning-tree shows it back to Desg/FWD.
  • Syslog logs the recovery event ('ROOTGUARD_UNBLOCK' or similar).

Contrast with BPDU Guard: BPDU Guard puts the port in err-disabled, which requires manual shut/no-shut or errdisable recovery config. Root Guard recovers on its own — no intervention needed once the threat goes away.

hack.memory: Root Guard = 'self-healing.' When the threat (superior BPDU) disappears, the port comes back on its own. No errdisable cleanup needed. Contrast: BPDU Guard = err-disabled, needs manual recovery.
hack.practice: Trigger Root Guard in Packet Tracer (see 2.5.d.1). While the port is root-inconsistent, run 'show spanning-tree inconsistentports' — port is listed. Now raise the offending switch's priority back to 32768 so it stops sending superior BPDUs. Wait ~20-30s. Port recovers automatically; the command above now returns 'No such ports found'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'how does a Root Guard port recover?' = automatically when superior BPDUs stop (no command needed).

2.5.d.info:
The configuration command that enables Root Guard. Applied under an interface (usually a Designated Port). Cisco-specific; there's no global Root Guard equivalent to spanning-tree portfast default — you turn it on per interface where needed.

Syntax:

interface gi0/1
 spanning-tree guard root

Remove it: no spanning-tree guard root — reverts the port to no Root Guard protection.

Placement strategy: Apply to designated-facing ports where you control the topology and want to prevent any downstream switch from hijacking root. Common spots:

  • Distribution switch → access switch links
  • Core switch → partner/vendor uplinks
  • Any port where someone could physically connect an unauthorized switch

Do NOT apply to: Ports facing your intended root path. Root Guard would block those if the root changes legitimately.

Verify on a specific port: show spanning-tree interface gi0/1 detail — look for 'Root guard is enabled'. Or check syslog for any 'ROOTGUARD_BLOCK' events.

Exam note: Know the exact command syntax (spanning-tree guard root) and that it's per-interface. Don't confuse with spanning-tree guard loop — that's Loop Guard, a different feature (and not part of the CCNA 200-301 v1.1 blueprint).

hack.memory: Command is literally: 'spanning-tree guard root' — applied under the interface. No global default. Put it on Designated ports where you control STP and don't trust the other side to NOT become root.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, enable 'spanning-tree guard root' on a distribution switch's downlink to an access switch. Verify with 'show spanning-tree interface X detail' — should say 'Root guard is enabled'. Then test the trip/recovery cycle from 2.5.d.1.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what is the command to enable Root Guard on a port?' = spanning-tree guard root (under the interface).

2.5.d.info:
Two STP protection features that look similar but solve different problems. The exam will test the distinction.

BPDU Guard:

  • Where: Access ports (PortFast).
  • Triggers on: ANY BPDU received.
  • Action: Port goes err-disabled (fully shut down).
  • Recovery: Manual (shut/no-shut) OR configured auto-recovery (errdisable recovery cause bpduguard).
  • Use case: Protect user-facing ports from rogue switches/hubs.

Root Guard:

  • Where: Designated Ports facing controlled downstream switches.
  • Triggers on: Superior BPDU only (one advertising a better Bridge ID than current root).
  • Action: Port goes root-inconsistent (blocked but not admin-down).
  • Recovery: Automatic when superior BPDUs stop.
  • Use case: Preserve your intended root bridge topology when trusting other switches to participate in STP but not become root.

Mental model: BPDU Guard is totalitarian — no BPDUs allowed, period. Root Guard is selective — BPDUs are fine, but you can't threaten the root.

Can you use both on the same switch? Yes, on different ports. BPDU Guard on access ports, Root Guard on trusted switch-facing ports. Never on the same port — they solve different scenarios.

Exam shortcut: 'err-disabled' + 'any BPDU' = BPDU Guard. 'root-inconsistent' + 'superior BPDU' = Root Guard.

hack.memory: BPDU Guard = 'any BPDU → err-disabled.' Used on access ports with PortFast. Manual recovery. Root Guard = 'superior BPDU → root-inconsistent.' Used on Designated ports to protect root election. Auto recovery. Key words: err-disabled vs root-inconsistent.
hack.practice: Write the matrix from scratch on paper: Feature | Port Type | Trigger | Action | Recovery. Fill in BPDU Guard and Root Guard. Quiz yourself until you can recite it in 10 seconds. This single table covers every exam question on STP guards.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam will test the distinction directly: 'which guard err-disables on any BPDU?' = BPDU Guard. 'Which only reacts to superior BPDUs?' = Root Guard. 'Which recovers automatically?' = Root Guard.

2.5.d.info:
Root-inconsistent is the blocking state Root Guard places a port in when it receives a superior BPDU. The port remains up physically, continues participating in STP (still sending BPDUs), but does NOT forward data frames — effectively behaving like an Alternate Port until the threat is removed.

Key distinctions from other states:

  • Different from err-disabled: err-disabled is a line-protocol-down state; root-inconsistent is STP-level only (port shows up in show interfaces as up/up).
  • Different from blocking/discarding: those are normal STP states from the protocol's role calculation; root-inconsistent is a protection override.
  • Different from shutdown: admin shutdown is fully disabled; root-inconsistent still processes BPDUs.

Verification commands:

  • show spanning-tree inconsistentports — lists all ports currently in an STP-inconsistent state (root-inconsistent, loop-inconsistent, etc.). Fast way to spot Root Guard trips.
  • show spanning-tree interface gi0/1 detail — shows the specific reason.
  • Syslog: look for 'ROOTGUARD_BLOCK' and 'ROOTGUARD_UNBLOCK' messages.

Recovery: Automatic — when superior BPDUs stop arriving (threat removed), the port waits out Max Age (~20s classic / faster RSTP), then transitions back to its original role (usually Designated/Forwarding).

Exam nuance: Root-inconsistent is specifically Root Guard's state name. Don't confuse with 'loop-inconsistent' (Loop Guard — not on the CCNA blueprint).

hack.memory: Root-inconsistent = Root Guard's block state. Port stays up/up in 'show interfaces' but doesn't forward data. NOT err-disabled. Self-heals when superior BPDUs stop. Find with 'show spanning-tree inconsistentports'.
hack.practice: Trigger Root Guard in Packet Tracer. Run 'show interfaces status' — port is still 'connected'. Run 'show spanning-tree' — port shows role Desg but state BKN*/inconsistent. Run 'show spanning-tree inconsistentports' — port is listed. Clear the threat; all three commands return to normal.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what STP state does a Root Guard-protected port enter after receiving a superior BPDU?' = root-inconsistent.

2.5.e.info:
The Bridge ID (BID) is the 8-byte identifier every switch uses during STP root bridge election. It's the single number that decides who becomes root: lowest Bridge ID wins.

Anatomy (8 bytes total):

  • 4-bit Bridge Priority — the administratively configurable piece. Default 32768, valid values 0-61440 in multiples of 4096.
  • 12-bit Extended System ID — the VLAN number of the STP instance. Cisco introduced this so the Bridge ID can be unique per VLAN without needing a separate MAC per VLAN.
  • 48-bit base MAC address — the switch's base MAC from its backplane. Tiebreaker only.

Combined priority you'll see in output: Cisco displays 'Priority' as Priority + Extended System ID summed. So a switch with priority 32768 participating in VLAN 10 shows combined priority 32778 (32768 + 10). This trips up new engineers who see the 32778 value and think it's wrong — it's actually Priority + VLAN ID.

Comparison rules for election:

  • Compare Priority (including extended sys ID) — lower wins.
  • If tied, compare base MAC — lower wins.

Verification: show spanning-tree vlan 10 shows both the Root ID (whoever is root) and the Bridge ID (this switch). Compare the two — if they match, this switch is root.

hack.memory: Bridge ID = Priority (4 bits) + VLAN number (12 bits) + MAC (48 bits) = 8 bytes. Lowest BID wins root election. Priority default 32768. Cisco shows 'Priority 32778' for VLAN 10 = 32768 + 10 (Extended System ID baked in).
hack.practice: Run 'show spanning-tree vlan 1' on any Cisco switch. Find the 'Priority' field — it'll show something like 32769 (= 32768 + 1 for VLAN 1). Now 'spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 4096'. Re-check — shows 4097 (= 4096 + 1). The 'off-by-VLAN' pattern is the Extended System ID at work.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'what are the three components of a Bridge ID?' = priority + Extended System ID (VLAN) + MAC. 'Default priority?' = 32768.

2.5.e.info:
The default STP Bridge Priority on Cisco switches is 32768. Every switch in every VLAN starts here unless an admin changes it. Combined with the Extended System ID (VLAN number), the combined priority value shown in show spanning-tree becomes 32768 + VLAN_ID — e.g., 32769 for VLAN 1, 32778 for VLAN 10, 32868 for VLAN 100.

Why 32768? It's the middle of the 4-bit priority range (0-61440 in 4096 steps). Leaves room to go lower (become root) or higher (avoid root) explicitly. Also matches the IEEE 802.1D default.

Valid priority values: Must be a multiple of 4096. Valid: 0, 4096, 8192, 12288, 16384, 20480, 24576, 28672, 32768, 36864, 40960, 45056, 49152, 53248, 57344, 61440. Try any other number — IOS rejects it.

Why multiples of 4096? The priority field is effectively 4 bits (the remaining 12 bits are used for the Extended System ID / VLAN). 2^12 = 4096 — each step in priority is 4096.

Problem with leaving defaults: All switches start at 32768. The tiebreaker becomes base MAC. The switch with the lowest MAC wins root — which is almost always the oldest switch in the network (MACs were allocated chronologically). That's usually an access-layer switch sitting in a closet, not where you want traffic to converge.

Fix: Always explicitly set the root. spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary sets priority to 24576, guaranteeing it beats defaults. spanning-tree vlan 10 root secondary sets 28672 for a backup.

hack.memory: Default priority = 32768. Valid priorities = multiples of 4096 (0, 4096, 8192, ..., 61440). If everyone defaults, the oldest switch (lowest MAC) wins root by accident — usually the wrong choice. Always set root explicitly.
hack.practice: Cisco switch: 'show spanning-tree vlan 1' — note Priority = 32769 (= 32768 + 1). Try 'spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 4000' — rejected, must be multiple of 4096. Use 4096 instead — now priority is 4097 and this switch is root.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'what is the default Bridge Priority?' = 32768. 'What values are allowed?' = multiples of 4096.

2.5.e.info:
STP priority on Cisco switches must be a multiple of 4096. IOS actively rejects any other value with an error like % Bridge Priority must be in increments of 4096. This trips up newcomers who try values like 5000 or 10000.

Why 4096? The Bridge ID has a fixed layout: 4 priority bits + 12 Extended System ID bits + 48 MAC bits. The Extended System ID slot (12 bits) carries the VLAN number (0-4095). The priority field lives in the upper 4 bits of the combined 16-bit priority word. 2^12 = 4096, so each priority step (each 'click' of the 4-bit priority) represents 4096 in the underlying 16-bit value.

Valid priority values (memorize the pattern, not all 16):

  • 0 (most aggressive — always root)
  • 4096 (next step — also very likely to become root)
  • 8192
  • 12288
  • 16384
  • 20480
  • 24576 — the value 'root primary' macro sets
  • 28672 — the value 'root secondary' macro sets
  • 32768 — default
  • ... up to 61440

Common real-world choices: 4096 or 8192 for primary root, 8192 or 16384 for secondary. 'root primary' / 'root secondary' macros pick 24576/28672 which still beat defaults but leave room for manual overrides.

Exam trap: A question may offer non-multiples of 4096 as distractors. Reject anything that isn't 0, 4096, 8192, 12288, etc.

hack.memory: Priority must be multiple of 4096. Valid: 0, 4096, 8192, 12288, ..., 61440. 32768 = default. 24576 = root primary macro. 28672 = root secondary macro. Try 'priority 5000' = IOS rejects.
hack.practice: On a Cisco switch, attempt 'spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 1000' — you'll get an error. Try 'spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 4096' — accepted. Memorize the three key values: 24576 (primary), 28672 (secondary), 32768 (default).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which of these are valid STP priorities?' distractors will include non-multiples of 4096 — reject them. Know 24576 and 28672 by heart.

2.5.e.info:
The root bridge election is a single-line algorithm: lowest Bridge ID wins, compared in order Priority → MAC. Every switch starts by claiming root; as BPDUs propagate, each switch learns whether someone has a better BID and stops claiming if so. When the dust settles, one switch has the lowest BID and is root.

Step-by-step:

  • Every switch begins by sending BPDUs with its own Bridge ID and claiming to be root (Root ID = own BID).
  • A switch receiving a BPDU with a lower Bridge ID than its own updates its 'known root' to the lower BID and stops originating its own claim.
  • Eventually all switches converge on agreement: the switch with the globally-lowest Bridge ID is root.

Comparison order:

  • Compare Priority (including Extended System ID) — lower wins.
  • If Priorities tie, compare base MAC — lower wins.

Since Priority is administratively controlled, it virtually always decides the election. MAC is only the tiebreaker when admins forget to set priorities.

Per-VLAN election: With PVST+/Rapid PVST+, the election repeats independently for each VLAN. Thanks to Extended System ID, a switch can have different effective Bridge IDs per VLAN (32768+1 for VLAN 1, 32768+10 for VLAN 10, etc.), and a different switch can win each VLAN's election.

Gotcha: Lower MAC = better. Cisco MACs are in hex; compare them as 48-bit numbers, not lexicographically. 00:1a:2b:... beats 00:1b:00:....

hack.memory: Election algorithm: lowest Priority wins → tie → lowest MAC wins. Nothing else matters. Per-VLAN with Rapid PVST+, so one switch can be root for VLAN 10 while another is root for VLAN 20. Always explicitly set priorities so MAC never becomes the tiebreaker.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, place three switches with default priorities. Whichever has the lowest MAC wins root (check with 'show spanning-tree'). Now set 'spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 4096' on a different switch — that one becomes root regardless of MACs. Priority always trumps MAC when not tied.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'which switch becomes the root bridge?' — always check priority first, then MAC. Memorize the two-step algorithm cold.

2.5.e.info:
Two convenience macros for setting STP priority on Cisco switches: spanning-tree vlan X root primary and spanning-tree vlan X root secondary. They let you designate a primary and backup root without hand-picking priority numbers.

spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary: Sets this switch's priority for VLAN 10 to 24576 (or 4096 less than the current lowest priority in the VLAN, whichever is lower). The goal: ensure this switch wins root election. Default 24576 comfortably beats the 32768 defaults.

spanning-tree vlan 10 root secondary: Sets priority to 28672. This switch becomes backup root — if the primary (24576) fails, this switch (28672) is next-lowest and takes over. Still beats everyone at default 32768.

Typical deployment: In a two-distribution-switch design:

Dist1: spanning-tree vlan 1-50 root primary
        spanning-tree vlan 51-100 root secondary
Dist2: spanning-tree vlan 51-100 root primary
        spanning-tree vlan 1-50 root secondary

This gives per-VLAN load sharing: Dist1 is root for odd VLANs, Dist2 for even. Both are backup root for the other's VLANs. If one fails, the other automatically becomes root for all VLANs.

Warning — macro vs explicit priority: The macros are one-time calculations at the moment you run them. If network conditions change later (a new switch joins with a lower priority), the macros don't auto-adjust. For maximum control, use spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096 explicitly instead.

Verification: After running the macro, show running-config | include priority shows the actual numeric priority the macro set (24576 or 28672).

hack.memory: 'root primary' = 24576, 'root secondary' = 28672. Both beat defaults (32768). Typical two-distribution design: flip primary/secondary across VLANs for load sharing. Macros are one-shot — they don't auto-adjust later.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: on SwA, 'spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary'. Check priority with 'show running-config | include priority' — it'll be 24576. On SwB, 'spanning-tree vlan 1 root secondary' — priority 28672. Now SwA is root; shut SwA and SwB takes over as root within seconds (RSTP) or 30s (classic).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'what priority does root primary set?' = 24576. 'root secondary?' = 28672. 'What are they called?' = root macros.

2.5.e.info:
Three ways to influence root bridge election on Cisco switches — know all three for the exam.

1. Priority macro (high-level intent):

spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 10 root secondary

Sets priority to 24576 or 28672. Easy, readable, good for typical designs.

2. Explicit priority (maximum control):

spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096

Sets an exact priority value. Must be a multiple of 4096 (0, 4096, 8192, ..., 61440). This is the command you use when the macros don't give you enough granularity — e.g., you need priority 4096 to beat another switch that someone already set to 24576.

3. Per-port cost (influence root port selection, not root election):

interface gi0/1
 spanning-tree cost 10
 spanning-tree vlan 10 cost 10

Doesn't change who's root, but changes which port becomes the root port on this switch by altering the STP path cost. Useful when you have multiple uplinks and want to force one as the preferred root path.

Verification commands:

  • show spanning-tree vlan 10 — compare Root ID vs Bridge ID sections.
  • show spanning-tree root — compact view of root per VLAN.
  • show running-config | section spanning-tree — all STP config.

Exam cheat sheet: 'priority' command takes numeric value (must be multiple of 4096). 'root primary/secondary' are macros. Per-interface 'spanning-tree cost' changes port cost, not priority.

left.label: Change who is root
items: spanning-tree vlan X priority N (multiple of 4096)

right.label: Change which port is root port

hack.memory: Three STP knobs: 'root primary/secondary' (macro → 24576/28672), 'priority N' (exact, multiples of 4096), 'spanning-tree cost N' per interface (changes root port selection, not root election). Know all three command forms.
hack.practice: Lab all three in Packet Tracer. 'root primary' vs 'priority 4096' — both make you root, but explicit gives lower value. Then on the resulting non-root switch, 'spanning-tree cost 10' on one uplink to force it as root port instead of the other. Verify with 'show spanning-tree'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 20-22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 9-10. Exam will test each command form. Common question: 'which command sets priority to 4096 for VLAN 10?' = spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096.

2.5.f.info:
The STP path cost assigned to each link type determines how switches rank paths to the root bridge. Faster link = lower cost. The IEEE 802.1D 'short' cost method (the default on most Cisco switches) uses these values — memorize them cold for the exam.

Short-method cost table:

  • 10 Gbps → cost 2
  • 1 Gbps → cost 4
  • 100 Mbps → cost 19
  • 10 Mbps → cost 100

Say it aloud right now: 2 - 4 - 19 - 100. That's every cost value tested on the CCNA.

Why these numbers? The 'short' method uses a 16-bit cost field, so values max at 65535. IEEE reverse-engineered the scale to fit 10 Mbps at a reasonable midpoint (100) and kept the ratios roughly inversely proportional to bandwidth.

Long method (informational only — not heavily tested): For higher-speed links (40G, 100G), IEEE introduced a 32-bit 'long' cost method with different values (e.g., 100G = 20, 10G = 2000, 1G = 20000). Cisco supports toggling with spanning-tree pathcost method long but the default on most hardware stays short. The CCNA tests the short method — know 2-4-19-100.

Verify configured cost on a port:

show spanning-tree vlan 10 | include Cost
show spanning-tree interface gi0/1

The 'Cost' column will show the value. 1G port = 4 by default; 100M port = 19 by default.

Override cost manually: interface gi0/1 + spanning-tree cost 10. Used to influence root port selection on a switch with multiple uplinks.

hack.memory: Cost table = '2-4-19-100.' 10G=2, 1G=4, 100M=19, 10M=100. Chant it until you can recall in a second. Faster = lower cost. These four numbers cover every STP cost question on the CCNA.
hack.practice: On any switch run 'show spanning-tree' and look at the Cost column. A Gi port should show cost 4 (1 Gbps). Fa port = 19 (100 Mbps). If you see different values, someone configured 'spanning-tree cost' manually or you're on 'long' method. Override with 'spanning-tree cost 100' under an interface and watch it change.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9 has the table. Exam will hand you link speeds and expect you to sum costs. Memorize 2-4-19-100 — non-negotiable.

2.5.f.info:
The total path cost is the sum of STP link costs along a path from a switch to the root bridge. It's the metric used to determine which port becomes the Root Port on each non-root switch.

How costs accumulate: Each switch adds the cost of the outgoing link when it receives a BPDU and re-advertises. So a switch two hops from root via 1G links has total cost 4 (first 1G) + 4 (second 1G) = 8. Root Port = the port with the lowest total cost; ties broken by neighbor Bridge ID, then neighbor port priority, then port number.

Worked example: Three switches: Root, SwA, SwB.

  • SwA → Root: direct 1G link. Total cost to root = 4. SwA picks this port as Root Port.
  • SwB → Root: directly via 1G link. Total cost = 4.
  • SwB → SwA → Root: two 1G links. Total cost = 4 + 4 = 8.

SwB's Root Port is the direct 1G to Root (cost 4, less than 8). The path through SwA becomes an Alternate Port.

Mixed speeds: If SwB has a 100M link direct (cost 19) and a 1G via SwA (cost 8), the 1G-via-SwA path wins (8 < 19). Speed per link matters, but the SUM determines the winner.

Key in output: show spanning-tree vlan 10 — the top section shows 'Cost' next to Root ID — that's the total path cost from this switch to root. Per-port costs (individual link cost) appear in the interface table's 'Cost' column.

Exam pattern: Given a topology with mixed link speeds, calculate total cost to root via each possible path, pick lowest for each switch's Root Port.

hack.memory: Total path cost = sum of all link costs from this switch to root. Root Port = lowest total cost port. Calculate per path, pick lowest. Speed per link doesn't matter — only the sum.
hack.practice: Draw a 4-switch diamond on paper. Set root at top. Mix link speeds: top-left 1G (cost 4), top-right 100M (cost 19), diagonals 1G (cost 4). For the bottom switch, calculate total cost via each of the three paths to root. Lowest = Root Port. Verify in Packet Tracer.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam will hand you a diagram with link speeds; you sum them and pick the winner. Worth practicing 5-10 topologies until cost math is automatic.

2.5.f.info:
When two or more ports on a switch have the same total path cost to the root, STP applies a tiebreaker chain to pick the Root Port. Knowing the order is required for the exam.

Tiebreaker chain (apply in order — stop at the first decisive step):

  • Step 1: Lowest total path cost to root. Decided; pick that port. If tied, continue.
  • Step 2: Lowest sender (upstream neighbor) Bridge ID. Which of the two potential RPs hears BPDUs from the better (lower-BID) upstream switch? That port wins.
  • Step 3: Lowest sender port priority. Default port priority is 128 (range 0-240, increments of 16). Lower port priority on the upstream side wins.
  • Step 4: Lowest sender port number. E.g., upstream Gi0/1 beats upstream Gi0/2.

Note: These tiebreakers look at the upstream neighbor's view, not this switch's ports. So Step 3 isn't 'my port priority' — it's 'the port priority on the other end of the wire'.

Configuration to influence tiebreakers:

  • Change cost directly: spanning-tree cost N (usually the cleanest fix).
  • Change port priority: spanning-tree port-priority N (on the upstream switch). Lower = preferred.

Why the tiebreakers matter: In a parallel-links design (two cables between the same two switches), costs and neighbor BIDs tie. Port number becomes the tiebreaker — and you might not like that the random lower port number wins. Override with port-priority to pick which cable you actually want as the root path.

hack.memory: Tiebreaker chain: Cost → Neighbor BID → Neighbor Port Priority → Neighbor Port Number. Mnemonic: 'Cost Breaks, Bid Beats, Priority Picks, Port Number Plays last.' Always compared from the UPSTREAM side, not yours.
hack.practice: Build a lab with two parallel 1G links between SwA and SwB, where SwB is the non-root. Both uplinks have cost 4 and same neighbor BID. Tiebreaker falls to port number — Gi0/1 wins over Gi0/2 on SwA's side. Now run 'spanning-tree port-priority 64' on SwA's Gi0/2 interface — that flips SwB's Root Port to the 0/2 side.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 21. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9 has the tiebreaker chain in detail. Exam favorite: 'two ports have equal cost to root — which becomes RP?' Answer requires knowing all four tiebreakers.

2.5.f.info:
The interface command to manually override STP path cost on a specific port. Used to influence root port selection without changing priorities or physical link speeds.

Syntax:

interface gi0/1
 spanning-tree cost 10

Or per-VLAN (with Rapid PVST+):

interface gi0/1
 spanning-tree vlan 10 cost 10

The per-VLAN form lets you tune STP paths on a per-VLAN basis — important because different VLANs may have different root bridges and should take different physical paths.

Valid range: 1 to 200000000 (200 million). Default values follow the short cost method (1G=4, 100M=19, etc.) unless overridden.

Typical use cases:

  • Force a specific uplink as the preferred root path (lower the cost on the desired port).
  • Force away from a specific link (raise the cost to make it unattractive).
  • Create asymmetric STP costs for load sharing across multiple VLANs (lower cost for VLAN 10 on path A, lower cost for VLAN 20 on path B).

Watch out: Cost is evaluated before neighbor Bridge ID tiebreakers. Changing cost is the most direct way to control port selection. Changing priorities only matters if costs tie.

Verify: show spanning-tree interface gi0/1 shows the current cost. show running-config interface gi0/1 shows the configured override.

Exam: Know the exact command syntax: spanning-tree cost [value] under the interface. Per-VLAN form is spanning-tree vlan X cost [value].

hack.memory: 'spanning-tree cost N' under an interface = manually set STP cost. Lower cost = preferred. Per-VLAN variant: 'spanning-tree vlan X cost N' — tune paths per VLAN for load sharing. Default cost follows link speed (4 for 1G, 19 for 100M).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer with two uplinks from SwA to root, default cost on both is 4. 'show spanning-tree' — one port is RP by port-number tiebreaker. Now 'interface gi0/1' + 'spanning-tree cost 10'. Re-check — the OTHER uplink (cost still 4) becomes RP because it's now cheaper. Cost override wins over tiebreakers.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 21. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam: 'how do you force a different port to become Root Port?' = lower its STP cost with 'spanning-tree cost N'.

2.5.g.info:
show spanning-tree is the single most important STP verification command. It displays the entire spanning-tree state: which switch is root, this switch's Bridge ID, all port roles and states, costs, and timers. You'll run it dozens of times per lab session and it appears on virtually every STP exam sim.

Output structure (per VLAN):

  • Header: protocol (rstp vs stp), VLAN number.
  • Root ID section: the root bridge's Priority (with Extended System ID) and MAC. If you are root, shows 'This bridge is the root'. Also shows hello/max-age/forward-delay timers as reported by the root.
  • Bridge ID section: this switch's own Priority and MAC. Compare to Root ID — if they match, this is the root.
  • Interface table: each port's Role, State (Sts), Cost, Priority.Port number, and Type.

Common variants:

  • show spanning-tree vlan 10 — just VLAN 10
  • show spanning-tree interface gi0/1 — just this port
  • show spanning-tree summary — quick overview of root per VLAN
  • show spanning-tree root — compact root info across all VLANs
  • show spanning-tree inconsistentports — ports currently tripped by guards
  • show spanning-tree detail — verbose per-port stats (BPDU counters, timer state)

Exam skill: Read the output and extract: (1) who is root, (2) what is my root port, (3) which ports are blocked, (4) what is my total cost to root. Practice until you can answer in under 5 seconds.

left.label: Top of output
items: Cost: total path cost to root from THIS switch
Bridge ID: my own priority + MAC
'This bridge is the root' if applicable

hack.memory: 'show spanning-tree' = the universal STP X-ray. Top shows root (Root ID) + this switch (Bridge ID); bottom shows per-port role/state/cost/type. Know the variants: vlan X, interface X, summary, root, inconsistentports, detail. Most common exam variant = 'show spanning-tree'.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer build a 4-switch topology. On every switch run 'show spanning-tree' and extract: (1) am I root? (2) which port is my RP? (3) which ports are Altn/BLK? (4) what's my cost to root? Do this drill until you can answer all four for any switch in under 5 seconds per switch.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 20-22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 9-10. This command is guaranteed on the exam. Sim/simlet scenarios rely on interpreting its output. Worth 3-5 exam questions directly.

2.5.g.info:
A specific line you'll see in show spanning-tree output: 'This bridge is the root' appears in the Root ID section of a VLAN when the switch you're logged into IS the root bridge for that VLAN. It's the fastest way to confirm root status.

Where it appears:

Switch# show spanning-tree vlan 10
VLAN0010
 Spanning tree enabled protocol rstp
 Root ID  Priority    24586
          Address     0011.2233.4455
          This bridge is the root    ← HERE
          Hello Time 2 sec Max Age 20 sec Forward Delay 15 sec

What it confirms: The Root ID and Bridge ID in the output will be identical (same priority + same MAC). STP has elected this switch as the root for the specified VLAN, and it's now originating BPDUs every hello interval.

If the line is absent: This switch is NOT root. The Root ID section shows a different priority/MAC (the actual root) and a Cost value representing the total path cost to reach it. The Bridge ID section below shows this switch's own priority/MAC.

Fastest root-verification technique: After running spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary, immediately run show spanning-tree vlan 10. If the line isn't there, your priority change didn't take effect (maybe another switch has an even lower priority), or you set it for the wrong VLAN.

Exam pattern: A simlet shows the command output and asks 'is this switch the root?' Look for the line. Present = yes. Absent = no.

nodes: Priority + MAC + 'This bridge is the root'

hack.memory: 'This bridge is the root' in 'show spanning-tree' output = you ARE the root. Absent = you're not. Fastest root-check in the game. Always re-run 'show spanning-tree' after a 'root primary' command to confirm.
hack.practice: After running 'spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary' in Packet Tracer, immediately run 'show spanning-tree vlan 1'. Scroll to Root ID section — the line should be present. Log into another switch and run the same command — line absent (someone else is root). Compare Bridge IDs to verify.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 20. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Exam simlets use this line to indicate root status — spot it fast and you'll save seconds per question.

2.5.g.info:
In show spanning-tree output, the Role column uses abbreviations for each port's STP role. Learn them so you can parse output instantly.

The four port role abbreviations:

  • Root (sometimes 'Root' written out, sometimes just 'R') — this port is the Root Port (lowest-cost path to root). Always in FWD state. Exactly one per non-root switch.
  • Desg — Designated Port. Forwards traffic toward root on behalf of a segment. Always FWD. All ports on the root bridge show Desg.
  • Altn — Alternate Port (RSTP). Hears a better BPDU via another switch; provides backup root-port path. BLK state.
  • Back — Backup Port (RSTP). Backs up a DP on the same switch. Extremely rare on modern hardware. BLK state.

Classic STP equivalent (older output): You may see 'Non-Desg' or 'Altr' for non-designated ports in classic PVST output. Modern Rapid PVST+ uses Altn/Back.

Example output row:

Gi0/1    Root    FWD    4      128.1    P2p
Gi0/2    Desg    FWD    4      128.2    P2p
Gi0/3    Altn    BLK    4      128.3    P2p

Reading this: Gi0/1 is Root Port (forwarding, cost 4 to root). Gi0/2 is Designated (forwarding). Gi0/3 is Alternate (blocked — backup path).

Exam skill: Instantly recognize each abbreviation. Sim questions will ask 'which port is the Root Port?' or 'which ports are blocked?' — you find them by the Role column.

right.label: Meaning
items: Backup (backup DP on same switch — rare)

hack.memory: Port roles: Root / Desg / Altn / Back. 'Root' = my path to the king, ONE per non-root switch. 'Desg' = this side of this segment is closer to root. 'Altn' = backup root port via another switch. 'Back' = backup designated port on same switch (rare).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer STP labs, on every 'show spanning-tree' output, circle the Role column values and name each role out loud. Root/Desg/Altn should dominate; Back is uncommon. This builds visual pattern recognition for exam speed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 20-22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 9-10. Exam sims rely on identifying Role abbreviations instantly. Practice until automatic.

2.5.g.info:
In show spanning-tree output, the Sts (State) column shows one of three abbreviations for the port's RSTP state. These map to the three RSTP port states.

The three state abbreviations:

  • FWD — Forwarding. Fully operational. Forwards user data frames AND learns MAC addresses. Target state for Root Ports and Designated Ports.
  • LRN — Learning. Transitional state. Does NOT forward data yet, but DOES learn MAC addresses. Brief — you'll usually only catch this during topology change.
  • BLK — Blocking / Discarding. Does NOT forward data and does NOT learn MACs. Applies to Alternate/Backup ports in steady state, and to ports during initial convergence.

Note on BLK: Cisco kept 'BLK' as the abbreviation for convenience even though RSTP officially renamed this state to Discarding. If you see BLK in RSTP output, treat it as Discarding. The behavior is the same.

What you'll normally see in a stable topology:

  • Root Port: FWD
  • Designated Ports: FWD
  • Alternate Ports: BLK
  • Backup Ports: BLK (rare)

Transient states you might catch:

  • During a topology change, a port might briefly show LRN before moving to FWD.
  • After a shut/no-shut, the port goes BLK → (proposal/agreement) → FWD in RSTP.

Exam pattern: 'Which ports are blocking?' = find Sts=BLK. 'Which ports are forwarding?' = Sts=FWD. Combined with Role, you can describe the entire topology from one command.

hack.memory: Three states: FWD / LRN / BLK. FWD = forwarding + learning (normal active). LRN = learning only, transitional. BLK = discarding — no forward, no learn (Alternate/Backup in steady state).
hack.practice: Run 'show spanning-tree' — count ports in each state. Stable topology: most are FWD (RPs and DPs), one or two BLK (Alternates). Shut a Root Port and re-run quickly: might catch a port briefly in LRN during RSTP transition.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 20-22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 9-10. Exam: 'which state indicates a port is forwarding traffic?' = FWD. 'Which state is a port in when blocked for loop prevention?' = BLK.

2.5.g.info:
The Type column in show spanning-tree output tells you the link type of each port — which informs RSTP whether it can use proposal/agreement for fast convergence.

Three common Type values:

  • P2p — Point-to-point full-duplex link. RSTP uses proposal/agreement here. Most switch-to-switch links in modern networks are P2p.
  • Shr — Shared (half-duplex) medium. RSTP falls back to timer-based behavior on shared links because proposal/agreement requires a single neighbor. Rare today — mostly legacy hubs or half-duplex situations.
  • Edge (sometimes 'P2p Edge') — PortFast-enabled port. Treated as an endpoint port; skips all STP convergence states and goes straight to Forwarding on link-up. Expect to see this on access ports with spanning-tree portfast configured.

Why the Type matters:

  • P2p → RSTP fast convergence (proposal/agreement) works.
  • Shr → RSTP timer-based (slower).
  • Edge → No STP wait at all, instant forward.

Force the Type:

  • spanning-tree link-type point-to-point — force P2p regardless of duplex auto-detection.
  • spanning-tree link-type shared — force Shr.
  • spanning-tree portfast or spanning-tree portfast trunk — mark as Edge (access ports) or Network (trunks).

Exam: Know that P2p = point-to-point = fast RSTP convergence, Shr = shared = slower, Edge = PortFast (endpoint port, no STP delay).

hack.memory: Type column: P2p (point-to-point, RSTP fast), Shr (shared, slower), Edge (PortFast, instant). Modern switch-to-switch = P2p. PC-facing access ports = Edge.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, on a switch with 'spanning-tree portfast' on an access port, run 'show spanning-tree' — the Type for that port = Edge. On a trunk between switches, Type = P2p. Force shared with 'spanning-tree link-type shared' — Type = Shr.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'what does P2p mean in show spanning-tree output?' = point-to-point link, enables RSTP proposal/agreement.

2.5.g.info:
show spanning-tree summary is a quick overview command that compresses STP status into a few lines per VLAN. Perfect when you want a bird's-eye view of a switch with many VLANs without paging through full per-VLAN output.

What it shows:

  • STP mode (rapid-pvst, pvst, mst)
  • Root bridge for each VLAN (which switch)
  • Port counts in each state per VLAN (blocking / listening / learning / forwarding)
  • STP feature status (PortFast default, BPDU Guard default, EtherChannel misconfig guard, UplinkFast, BackboneFast, etc.)

Typical output:

Switch# show spanning-tree summary
Switch is in rapid-pvst mode
Root bridge for: VLAN0001, VLAN0010
Extended system ID                      is enabled
Portfast Default                        is disabled
Portfast Edge BPDU Guard Default        is disabled
UplinkFast                              is disabled
BackboneFast                            is disabled
Configured Pathcost method used is short

Name           Blocking Listening Learning Forwarding STP Active
---------------------- -------- --------- -------- ---------- ----------
VLAN0001                  0         0        0          5          5
VLAN0010                  1         0        0          4          5
----------------------- -------- --------- -------- ---------- ----------
2 vlans                   1         0        0          9         10

Quick-scan checks:

  • 'Root bridge for: VLAN X, Y' — which VLANs have us as root.
  • 'PortFast Default' / 'BPDU Guard Default' — whether global defaults are enabled.
  • Per-VLAN table — any Blocking ports? That's your Alternate Ports.

Related compact command: show spanning-tree root — shows the root bridge for each VLAN in a compact table. Great for verifying load sharing across distribution switches.

Exam: Know that 'summary' gives a high-level overview, 'root' shows root per VLAN, full 'show spanning-tree' dumps everything.

right.label: show spanning-tree root
items: Cost, root port, timers per VLAN

hack.memory: 'show spanning-tree summary' = bird's-eye overview (per-VLAN root, port-state counts, feature flags). 'show spanning-tree root' = compact root-per-VLAN table. Use these when full 'show spanning-tree' is too verbose.
hack.practice: On any Cisco switch with multiple VLANs, run all three: 'show spanning-tree', 'show spanning-tree summary', 'show spanning-tree root'. Compare — each answers different questions faster for different scenarios. Get comfortable picking the right one quickly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 22. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'which command gives a per-VLAN STP overview in one table?' = show spanning-tree summary.
